coredata.dc:title: Hyaluronic acid as a material for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and its application for selective detection of Fe3+ ion and folic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Qiu-Lin
Pu, Zheng-Fen
Yang, Yan-Ju
Wang, Jun
Wu, Bi-Chao
Hu, Yi-Lin
Liu, Peng
Ling, Jian
Cao, Qiue
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using hyaluronic acid as a carbon source was studied here. The results indicated that the addition of citric acid can improve the chemical stability and fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The citric acid plays a positive role in the synthesis of the CDs and prevent excessive carbonization of hyaluronic acid. Therefore, a bright blue carbon dots was obtained by one-step hydrothermal method using hyaluronic acid and citric acid. As a small number of ferric ions (Fe3+) and folic acid (FA) can quench the fluorescence of the carbon dots, and the fluorescence quenching ratio has a good linear relationship with Fe3+ and FA concentrations. Two fluorescence analysis methods for sensitive detection of Fe3+ and FA respectively were established. The different fluorescence quenching mechanisms of HCA-CDs toward the two targets respectively and the analytical parameters for the detection of the two targets were studied. The results indicated the proposed methods have the potential for sensing Fe3+ and FA in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrothermal method
Fe3+
Folic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and preparation of the fluorescence CDs
Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ using HCA-CDs
Fluorescence detection of folic acid using HCA-CDs
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of HCA-CDs towards Fe3+ and FA

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21565030), Program for Excellent Young Talents of Yunnan University, and National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Education (Yunnan University).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of HCA-CDs (A) and HA-CDs (B) their TEM images. Comparison of CDs synthesized at different condition. (C) UV absorption spectroscopy and photos of CDs synthesized by CA, HA and their mixture. (D) Fluorescence spectrum the three CDs and their photos under 365 nm of UV light.

Analytical parameters of the Fe3+ detection using HCA-CDs. (A) Selectivity. The concentration of all metal ions were 0.2 mM (B) Interference. The concentration of Fe3+ was 0.2 mM, and the concentration of other metal ions were 0.4 mM (C) Fluorescence quenching spectra. (D) Regression equation. (Inset: Photographs under 365 nm of UV light).

Analytical parameters of the FA detection using HCA-CDs. (A) Selectivity: The concentration of all biomolecules were 0.2 mM (B) Interference. The concentration of FA was 0.2 mM, and the concentration of other biomolecules were 0.4 mM (C) Fluorescence quenching spectra. (D) Regression equation. (Inset: Photographs under 365 nm of UV light).

The overlaps of the fluorescence spectrum of HCA-CDs with the absorption spectra of Fe3+ (A) and FA (B). Fluorescence decay curves of HCA-CDs in the presence of different amounts of Fe3+ (C) and FA (D).
Illustration of carbon dots synthesis and its application for ferric ion and folic acid detection.
Comparison of Fe3+ detection with previously reported fluorescence method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Vitamin B1- doped carbon quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the folic acid detection with previously reported fluorescence method.
Quantitation of Fe3+ in water sample.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Water samples 1, 2 and 3 are from the Panlong River in Kunming. N.Db means not detected.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantitation of FA in human urine sample.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Water samples 1, 2 and 3 are from the Volunteer's human urine. N.Db means not detected.

head.ce:title: Hyaluronic acid as a material for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and its application for selective detection of Fe3+ ion and folic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Molecules Analysis and Biotransformation Key Laboratory of Universities in Yunnan Province, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Education (Yunnan University), College of Chemical Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional Molecules Analysis and Biotransformation Key Laboratory of Universities in Yunnan Province
National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Education (Yunnan University)
College of Chemical Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Molecules Analysis and Biotransformation Key Laboratory of Universities in Yunnan Province, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Education (Yunnan University), College of Chemical Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using hyaluronic acid as a carbon source was studied here. The results indicated that the addition of citric acid can improve the chemical stability and fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The citric acid plays a positive role in the synthesis of the CDs and prevent excessive carbonization of hyaluronic acid. Therefore, a bright blue carbon dots was obtained by one-step hydrothermal method using hyaluronic acid and citric acid. As a small number of ferric ions (Fe3+) and folic acid (FA) can quench the fluorescence of the carbon dots, and the fluorescence quenching ratio has a good linear relationship with Fe3+ and FA concentrations. Two fluorescence analysis methods for sensitive detection of Fe3+ and FA respectively were established. The different fluorescence quenching mechanisms of HCA-CDs toward the two targets respectively and the analytical parameters for the detection of the two targets were studied. The results indicated the proposed methods have the potential for sensing Fe3+ and FA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of discrete and almost spherical “zero-dimensional” carbon nanomaterial with sizes below 10 nm, can be divided into three types based on their nature, quantum confinement and crystalline structure [1]. The surface of CDs is passivated and functionalized with organic or biomolecules, the shape is a monodispersed quasi-spherical [2]. Comparison to classic quantum dots and fluorescent molecules, CDs show numerous superior advantages such as environmentally friendly [3,4], excellent biocompatibility [5], simple synthesis route in and diversified fluorescence properties, which has become a substitute for traditional organic fluorescent dyes. The origin of the fluorescence of the carbon dots depends primarily on the carbon source, experimental conditions and functional groups [6]. CDs as nascent quantum dots (QDs) have been proven to contain different kinds of surface oxygen groups rather than pure carbon compositions. which inner part is composed of sp2‐hybridized carbon atoms while the outer part is sp3‐hybridized carbon atoms [7]. In fact, CDs contain many oxygen‐containing functional groups such as carboxylic acid moieties at their surface, thus making them possess excellent water solubility, easily tunable surface functionalization or subsequent labels with various polymeric or biological species [8]. Thus, they have good water solubility [9], low toxicity [10], physical and chemical stability [11], and have been widely used in biological imaging [12], fluorescent probe [13], drug delivery [14], photocatalytic [15], etc. These excellent applications have generated great interest in finding new methods for synthesis of high-quality CDs and exploring new applications in wide research fields.

Hyaluronic acid (HA), as one of the main body tissues, is a non-toxic and biodegradable natural acidic polysaccharide macromolecule with unique viscoelasticity and limited immunogenicity that binds to the CD44 receptors of cancer cells [16,17]. In addition, researches based on HA nanogels for bioimaging and drug delivery have attracted wide attention due to its excellent biocompatibility and rich chemical functions [18–21]. Subsequently, HA-derived carbon dots attract the interest of researchers [22–24]. Zhang et al. studied hyaluronic acid-derived multifunctional carbon dots for self-targeting imaging-guided photodynamic therapy [25]. Although HA-derived carbon dots were reported in bioimaging and drug delivery [26,27], there are no reports on the direct detection of small metal ions and biological molecules.

It is well known that ferrum is a very important metal element for the living being. Overload and deficiency of ferrum can induce serious physiological damage and biological obstacles, such as renal failure [28], heart disease [29], liver injury [30], and cancer [31]. Thus, the increase of Fe3+ content in environmental water has become a great threat to human health. Therefore, it is critical for human health monitoring to determine Fe3+ in biological, medical and environmental samples. In addition, the detection of folic acid is also very important in clinical medicine, because folic acid is usually used as a medicine for pregnant women to treat and prevent giant erythrocyte anemia [32]. Insufficient folate levels can damage cell division and cause fetal developmental defects [33]. Folic acid deficiency also leads to an increase in the level of homocysteine in the serum [34], which in turn causes cardiovascular diseases such as coronary heart disease and stroke [35]. Conventional qualitative and quantitative strategies for Fe3+ and folic acid detection required complex instrumentation and tedious sample preparation procedures, which limited their prosperous applications [36,37]. Thus, developing a sample analytical method for the rapid detection of Fe3+ and folic acid has attracted the attention of researchers.

In this paper, we developed an extremely green and economical strategy to produce high-luminescence CDs by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of hyaluronic acid (HA) with excellent water solubility. In this synthesis, hyaluronic acid was used as a precursor and citric acid was selected as an assistant to improve the fluorescence. The as-prepared carbon dots (named HCA-CDs) has a high fluorescence emission at 437 nm. Further studies found that the fluorescence of HCA-CDs could be selectively and sensitively quenched by Fe3+ and folic acid (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, the HCA-CDs was applied to detect the two targets in environmental water or biological system. We also intensively studied the fluorescence quenching mechanism of HCA-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ and folic acid by using measuring changes in fluorescence lifetime. The results indicated that the quenching of HCA-CDs by Fe3+ and FA was static and dynamic, respectively. Thus, we believed that the HCA-CDs were expected to be suitable candidates for Fe3+ and FA detection in samples.

The fluorescence spectra were measured using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photographs of the CDs used for visual fluorescence were captured by a Nikon D-7000 camera. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was taken on JEM-2100 (Japan Electronics, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) data were collected on a K-Alpha + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with an Al Kα = 1486.6 eV excitation source. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analyses were performed on a Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific) using KBr pellets. The pH values of the buffer solution were measured by PHS-25 pH meter (Shanghai Thunder Magnetic Instrument Factory, China).

The citric acid (analytical grade) was purchased from Tianjin Sailboat Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd. The hyaluronic acid (analytical grade) was purchased from Chengdu Aikeda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The CaCl2, CdSO4·8H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, KCl, NaCl, MgSO4·7H2O, SnCl2·2H2O, Pb(NO3)2, ZnSO4·7H2O, MnSO4·H2O, folic acid (FA), vitamin C (VC), glucose (Glc), glutathione (GSH), dopamine (Da), cysteine (Cys), phenylalanine (Phe), tryptophan (Trp), isoleucine (Iso), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met) and threonine (Thr) were all purchased Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd (Shanghai China). All reagents were used as received and without further purification. The 20 mM Britton-Robison (B-R) buffer solution was prepared by adjusting the volume of solution A (0.02 mol/L mixed acid: 3.92 g H3PO4, 2.36 mL acetic acid and 2.47 g H3BO3 in one liter of water) and solution B (0.02 mol/L NaOH). The 20 mM HAc-NaAc buffer solution was prepared by adjusting the volume of solution A (0.02 mol/L acetic acid) and solution B (0.02 mol /L sodium acetate) and used pH meter to measure the pH values of these mixed solutions. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ) was used for the preparation of all aqueous solutions.

The HCA-CDs were synthesized according to reported papers with modification [38]. Firstly, hyaluronic acid (5.0 mmol) and citric acid (12.5 mmol) were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water. After shaking for 5 min and ultrasonicated for 20 min, the clear solution was transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclaves and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the obtained brownish-yellow product was purified with a dialysis bag (Mw = 1000 Da) for 12 h to remove soluble impurities, and further filtrated with 0.2 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble impurities. Finally, the purified HCA-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

Take multiple groups of 200 μl HCA-CDs original solution, then, added different concentrations of Fe3+ solution into the above solution. Finally, reacted at room temperature for 30 min after diluting to 1.00 mL with HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 6.0, 20 mM). Following, measured the fluorescence intensity of different solutions. The fluorescence measurement setting conditions were an excitation wavelength of 362 nm, a maximum emission wavelength of 437 nm, a slit width of 5 nm, and a PMT voltage of 700 V. Metal ion selectivity and interference experiments are similar to above operations. The selectivity experiment was to measure the fluorescence intensity of the 200 μl HCA-CDs original solution after reacting with 0.2 mM various metal ions (Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe3+) for 30 min. For interference experiments, 0.4 mM other metal ions need to be added to the 200 μl HCA-CDs solution before adding Fe3+.

Take multiple groups of 200 μl HCA-CDs original solution, then, added different concentrations of FA solution into the above solution. Finally, reacted at room temperature for 30 min after diluting to 1.00 mL with HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 6.0, 20 mM). Following, measured the fluorescence intensity of different solutions. The fluorescence measurement setting conditions were an excitation wavelength of 362 nm, a maximum emission wavelength of 437 nm, a slit width of 5 nm, and a voltage of 700 V. FA selectivity and interference experiments are similar to above operations. The selectivity experiment was to measure the fluorescence intensity of the 200 μl HCA-CDs original solution after reacting with 0.2 mM various biological molecules (FA, VC, Glc, GSH, Da, Cys, Phe, Trp, Iso, Leu, Lys, Met, Thr) for 30 min. For interference experiments, 0.4 mM other biological molecules need to be added to the 200 μl HCA-CDs solution before adding FA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and preparation of the fluorescence CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, fluorescence CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal reaction using hyaluronic acid (HA) as a precursor. The role of citric acid (CA) in the CDs synthesis was also studied here as it has been reported to synthesized CDs directly at certain experimental conditions [39]. Interestingly, fluorescence CDs cannot be synthesized by citric acid alone at our experimental condition, but it can assist the synthesis of fluorescence CDs by HA that the fluorescence of CDs synthesized by HA and CD is nearly 5 times stronger than that by HA alone. Thus, the two fluorescence CDs synthesized by HA alone (named HA-CDs) and by HA and CA (named HCA-CDs) was studied here. The fluorescence spectrum of HCA-CDs measured is shown in Fig. 1 A. The maximum excitation and emission peak positions of HCA-CDs are at 362 nm and 437 nm, respectively. The morphology of the carbon dots was observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. TEM image of HCA-CDs illustrates that the size of HCA-CDs is distributed in the range from 4 to 6 nm (Fig. 1 inset), with average sizes of 4.2 nm (Fig. S1A). While, the fluorescence of HA-CDs is significantly lower than that of HCA-CDs (Fig. 1B). The maximum excitation and emission peak positions of HA-CDs were at 365 nm and 445 nm, respectively, Clear lattices can be seen from Fig. 1B inset of TEM image of HA-CDs, the average particle size is 4.7 nm (Fig. S1B). The results show that the carbon dots can also be synthesized using HA without adding other substances, and the presence of CA directly increases the fluorescence of CDs.

The effect of citric acid on the morphology of HA-CDs was first investigated here. According to the absorption spectrum in Fig. 1C, it can be found that there is an evident difference in the absorption peaks of CA, HA-CDs and HCA-CDs. In detail, the absorption peak of citric acid alone is almost negligible, and when citric acid is absent from the system, HA-CDs have a weak broad peak at 262 nm. However, the obtained HCA-CDs with obvious absorption peak around 280 nm when both citric acid and hyaluronic acid are present in the system. Fig. 1C inset shows the color of the three solutions synthesized in the control experiment under daylight. The product solution of HA-CDs is dark brown, while the HCA-CDs is yellow. The fluorescence was strongest only when HA and CA reacted together, and the HCA-CDs is bright blue under UV light (Fig. 1D). The HA-CDs show blue-green color due to a red-shift of 8 nm at the maximum emission peak in inset Fig. 1D. We suspected that citric acid has a role in protecting the complete carbonization of hyaluronic acid that the CDs solutions synthesized HA and CA is always clearer than that synthesized by HA alone.

In addition, to better understand the difference in the fluorescence properties of HA-CDs and HCA-CDs, more analytical characterization was carried out. It can be seen from two slopes in Fig. S2A that the fluorescence lifetime of HA-CDs is shorter than that of HCA-CDs. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. S2B showed that there was no significant difference between the main peaks of HA-CDs and HCA-CDs, indicating that there is no essential difference in the structure between the two carbon dots. The HA-CDs and HCA-CDs show peaks at 3427 cm−1 (the –OH stretching vibration of the carboxylic acid dimer), 1719 cm−1 (CO), 1216 cm−1(C-O-C), and a broad peak at 1348 cm−1(–CN). Therefore, it is understood that the HA-CDs and HCA-CDs contain functional groups such as hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups, and others, that is the reason why they are well water-soluble. However, it is worth noting that the signals of HA-CDs at 3427 cm−1 and 1719 cm−1 are not as strong as HCA-CDs, and the two peaks correspond to –OH stretching vibration and CO stretching. It can be speculated that the introduction of citric acid increases the content of –OH and CO in HCA-CDs. At the same time, similar results can be obtained from the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in Fig. S3 and S4. Comparing Fig. S3 and S4, the elemental and valence states of the analysis showed no difference in the valence of HA-CDs and HCA-CDs, but the CO peak signal of HCA-CDs was significantly stronger than HA-CDs, also demonstrating the CO content is increased with the introduction of citric acid in the system. The oxygen and carbon contents were 35.99% and 59.22% in HA-CDs and 41.13% and 57.00% in HCA-CDs, respectively.

As the solution of CDs is a mixture of many different sizes and shapes of single carbarn dot, the fluorescence emission position would shift with the moving of excitation wavelength. Thus, the fluorescence emission shifts of the two CDs were also studied by changing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm. As shown in Fig. S5, the maximum emission peak position of HA-CDs red-shifted 38 nm that is wider than that of HCA-CDs, which shifted 28 nm. The results indicated that HCA-CDs solution has better monodispersity that HA-CDs. To further check the point of view, the fluorescence stability of the two CDs in different pH (20 mM BR buffer) environments will also be studied. The data shown in Fig. S6 proved that viewpoint.

As the above characterization indicated that CA plays important roles on the stability and fluorescence property of the CDs, the mixture of CA and HA was used to synthesize fluorescence CDs, and the synthesis condition of HCA-CDs was optimized.

The effects of each raw material concentration, reaction time and temperature are shown in supplementary materials Fig. S7. The experimental results show that the optimization reaction temperature is 180 °C, and the optimization reaction time is 6 h. The concentrations of hyaluronic acid and citric acid are 5 mM and 12.5 mM respectively, that is, the molar concentration ratio of citric acid to hyaluronic acid is 5:1. The HCA-CDs prepared at optimization reaction was used to further study the sensing of metal ions and small biological molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ using HCA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the potential application of HCA-CDs for metal ions detection, the fluorescence responses of the HCA-CDs toward various metal ions, such as K+, Na+, Mg2+, Sn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+ are studied (Fig. 2 A, Fig. S8A). The results show that only Fe3+ can significant quench the fluorescence of HCA-CDs, indicating the high selectivity of HCA-CDs for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. Further experiments for the effect of coexisting metal ions on the quenched fluorescence intensity of HCA-CDs by Fe3+ are performed to show the interference of Fe3+ detection by HCA-CDs (Fig. 2B, Fig. S8B). The results indicated that the detection Fe3+ by HCA-CDs shows high anti-interference to other coexisting metal. The fluorescence of HCA-CDs is gradually quenched as the Fe3+ concentration increased (Fig. 2C). From Fig. 2D inset, as the concentration of Fe3+ increases, the blue fluorescence of the system under UV light gradually decreases. The fluorescence quenching rate (I0/I) of HCA-CDs had a good linear relationship to the Fe3+ concentration (R 2 = 0.99) in the concentration range of 0.1–900.0 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD, 3σ) of 0.008 μM (Fig. 2D), showing the high precision for Fe3+ detection on HCA-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of folic acid using HCA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As the well biocompatibility and friendlily of HA, the synthesized HCA-CDs was also used to study its potential application in biological small molecules detention. Similarly, the fluorescence responses of HCA-CDs toward various biological small molecules, such as common amino acids, dopamine, folic acid, glucose, glutathione, and ascorbic acid studied (Fig. 3 A, Fig. S9A). The results show that only folic acid (FA) gave significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of HCA-CDs, indicating the high selectivity of HCA-CDs for the detection and specific recognition of FA in aqueous solution. Further experiments for the effect of coexisting biomolecules on the quenched fluorescence intensity of HCA-CDs by FA are performed to show the interference of FA detection by HCA-CDs (Fig. 3B, Fig. S9B). It is very encouraging that the detection of FA by HCA-CDs shows high anti-interference to other coexisting biomolecules. The fluorescence quenching rate (I 0/I) of HCA-CDs had a good linear relationship to the FA concentration (R 2 = 0.99) in the concentration range of 4.0–100.0 μM FA (Fig. 3C and 3D) with a low limit of detection (LOD, 3σ) of 0.49 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of HCA-CDs towards Fe3+ and FA
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the similar fluorescence response of Fe3+ and FA to HCA-CDs, we studied the effect of the environment condition such as pH, ionic strength, reaction time and concentration of HCA-CDs on the two targets. The experimental results show that the effects of pH (Fig. S10) and salt concentration (Fig. S11) can be ignored during the detection of Fe3+. However, when the pH is greater than 5.0, FA has a better quenching effect on HCA-CDs. Therefore, after comprehensively considering the effect of pH on Fe3+ and FA, we all use BR buffer with pH 6.0 in the detection procedure. The kinetic behaviors shown in Fig. S12 has similar results that the fluorescence quenching process by Fe3+ and FA would be finished after 5 min of reaction. The above data showed similar results between the interaction of HCA-CDs with Fe3+ and FA.

To further exploring the reason for fluorescence quenching of HCA-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and FA were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the absorption spectra of Fe3+ have an overlap with the excitation spectrum of HCA-CDs. The spectral overlap reveals that the occurrence of the inner filter effect (IFE) when Fe3+ and HCA-CDs coexisted in solution. IFE describes the absorption of excitation or emission light of the fluorophores in a fluorescent detection system [40]. In addition, the absorption spectrum of FA fully overlapped the excitation spectrum of the HCA-CDs (Fig. 4B), and the excitation light of the HCA-CDs could be absorbed by FA with a high molar absorption coefficient. This suggested an efficient IFE between FA and HCA-CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the HCA-CDs. Besides, it can be seen from Fig. S13 that the absorption spectra of other metal ions and biomolecules do not overlap with the excitation spectra of HCA-CDs. This result again shows that the IFE is a non-negligible mechanism for fluorescence quenching of HCA-CDs by Fe3 + and FA.

To further explore the main cause of the fluorescence quenching, we also measured the lifetimes of the HCA-CDs before and after quenching. The fluorescence decay curves are shown in Fig. 4C and 4D indicating that the fluorescence lifetime of HCA-CDs did not change significantly with increasing Fe3+ concentration, while the fluorescence lifetime of HCA-CDs increases with the increasing of FA concentration. The results indicated that static quenching was occurred between HCA-CDs and Fe3+, while fluorescence quenching between HCA-CDs and FA is dynamic quenching. According to the previously reported papers [41,42], Fe3+ would coordinated with hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs to induce aggregation of CDs that cause fluorescence quenching of CDs. While, FA may dynamic quench the fluorescence of HCA-CDs by static electric interaction.

In order to further study whether a complex was formed between Fe3+ and HCA-CDs, we analyzed the FTIR and XPS spectra of the mixture of Fe3+ and HCA-CDs. Comparing the two lines a and b in Fig. S14 (FTIR), we can find the absorption of carboxyl peak signal is attenuated, while the high-resolution spectrum of Fig. S15C (XPS) shows two main peaks. The binding energy peak at 400.5 eV is attributed to the C-N bond, and the peak at about 397 eV can be attributed to the metal nitride. The results could not indicate the precise structure of Fe3+ binding to HCA-CDs, but could roughly indicate that a complex was formed between Fe3+ and HCA-CDs. We speculate that the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ is the synergy between the internal filtration effect and the formation of complex. However, the XPS (Fig. S16) spectra of FA and HCA-CDs mixtures have no chemical valence changes; it cannot be judged whether a complex is formed between the two. Thus, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon and mechanism of the HCA-CDs by Fe3+ and FA is similar but also has quite a difference in some aspect.

To further investigate the practicality and accuracy in a practical sample, we collected samples from a local river to test the reliability of the method for detecting Fe3+ by the standardized addition method. As shown in Table 3 , the results indicated that the method had a recovery from 98.1% to 106.4% for the detection of Fe3+ at higher concentration. For the real sample of FA, we collected the urine of three volunteers and performed the following pretreatment: Human urine was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for ten minutes, and the supernatant was taken and freeze-dried to obtain a yellow powder. The obtained yellow powder was ultrasonically dissolved in methanol, the insoluble matter was removed by centrifugation, and the supernatant was taken as the real FA sample. As shown in Table 4 , the method had a recovery from 107.2% to 108.8% for the detection of FA. The results indicated that the recovery rates of FA are high due to the interference of background absorption in the urine sample. Nevertheless, we believe that HCA-CDs has the potential for further application in other analytical fields.

We developed a fluorescent CDs material by hyaluronic acid for the highly sensitive and sensing of Fe3+ and FA. The synthesized CDs was characterized by TEM, ultraviolet–visible spectrum, fluorescence spectrum and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. It was found that the CDs contained functional groups such as hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups. The fluorescence at 438 nm of the CDs can be selectively quenched by Fe3+ and FA. The fluoresce quenching mechanisms of the CDs toward the two targets are different. Overall, the synthesis of the CDs is simple, efficient, and environmentally friendly and could be prepared without further chemical modification of the CDs. The proposed strategy provided satisfying analytical performance for Fe3+ and FA, indicating its potential application in biological detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21565030), Program for Excellent Young Talents of Yunnan University, and National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Education (Yunnan University).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105364.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Cadranel, J.T. Margraf, V. Strauss, T. Clark, D.M. Guldi, Carbon Nanodots for Charge-Transfer Processes, Acc Chem Res. 52 (2019) 955-963.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Liang, W. Ma, Y. Shi, Z. Li, X. Yang, Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications, Carbon. 60 (2013) 421-428.
